task_summary: Visual/UX review of R/Shiny migration â€” bslib theming, ggplot2 aesthetics, Chromebook layout
routing_reason: Same role as obs #2 â€” visual/aesthetic.

outcome.notes: Gemini delivered actionable, code-level R/Shiny recommendations. Went BEYOND visual critique into practical engineering: (1) hybrid plotly/ggplot2 strategy for interactivity, (2) thematic package for auto-CSS-to-ggplot2 inheritance, (3) client-side JS tab switching to avoid 100-200ms server roundtrip latency, (4) reactable over DT for dark-mode tables, (5) accordion+layout_sidebar for Pipeline stepper, (6) lazy rendering via req(input$nav), (7) SVG over PNG for projector crispness. Also proposed NEW educational features: 'Show R Code' buttons, raw summary(lm()) output for AP exam prep, PDF export for lab reports. Output was structured as a 6-section review with concrete code snippets throughout.

output_characteristics.format: 6 sections with tables, code snippets, and a summary checklist
output_characteristics.actionability: very high â€” every recommendation has working R code

task_summary: Systems architecture review of R/Shiny migration â€” Docker, Railway, SQLite IPC, process supervision
routing_reason: Same role as obs #1 â€” systems/architecture.

outcome.notes: GPT delivered the most thorough review of the three. Deeply researched with citations. Caught a CRITICAL issue CC and others missed: Python 3.13 is not available in Ubuntu 22.04 LTS repos used by rocker/r-ver, so apt-get install python3.13 will fail. Structured as 7 major sections with explicit risk/failure-mode analysis for each. Key findings: (1) Docker Python/R version mismatch â€” 3 viable routes proposed, (2) shell-as-PID1 won't propagate SIGTERM â€” supervisor with stopasgroup needed, (3) SQLite two-writer contention â€” enforce single-writer (Python only), (4) mixed-tick reads without transaction fencing, (5) Railway port/volume/build-timeout constraints as hard design inputs, (6) feature-flagged dual-UI period for rollback safety, (7) cross-session caching for classroom scalability. Every section has concrete implementation-level recommendations, not just observations.

output_characteristics.format: 7 sections, each with Assessment â†’ Risks â†’ Recommendations â†’ Priority
output_characteristics.actionability: very high â€” supervisor config knobs, PRAGMA settings, code patterns all specified
output_characteristics.research_depth: deep â€” cited Railway docs, SQLite docs, Rocker docs, Docker best practices
output_characteristics.meta_finding: new reliability coupling â€” bot and UI share container lifecycle, must decide which failures take down whole container vs self-heal

task_summary: Statistical accuracy review of R/Shiny migration â€” ggplot2 correctness, AP Stats conventions
routing_reason: Same role as obs #3 â€” mathematical precision.
cc_pre_identified_issue: geom_smooth(se=TRUE) gives confidence band for mean, not prediction interval.

outcome.notes: DeepSeek delivered exactly what was expected â€” precision mathematical audit with severity classifications. Confirmed CC's pre-identified issue (confidence band vs prediction interval) and classified it as INCORRECT. Structured as 10 sections covering every chart type. Used a consistent table format: Finding | Severity | Correct Approach | AP Stats Reference. Caught 7 critical issues: (1) prediction band confusion â€” CONFIRMED CC's prediction, (2) horizontal CI number lines need coord_flip, (3) two-sided shading incomplete, (4) geom_dotplot misrepresents continuous data, (5) residual plot x-axis should be fitted values not original x, (6) mosaic plot not AP-standard (replace with segmented bar), (7) axis labels incomplete. Each fix has exact R code. Also validated that boxplot quartile method is CORRECT (Tukey's hinges match AP). Severity classifications: INCORRECT, MISLEADING, INCOMPLETE, ENHANCEMENT, CORRECT, OK, MINOR.

output_characteristics.format: 10 sections with severity-coded tables, each row has Finding | Severity | Correct Approach | AP Stats Reference
output_characteristics.actionability: very high â€” exact R code for every fix

Changes categorized by source: Architecture (GPT) â€” r2u:24.04 base, supervisord mandatory, SIGTERM handler, loopback-only internal API, Railway Volume required. Data Transport (GPT) â€” single-writer enforced, recompute tables removed (HTTP replaces), atomic tick fence, heartbeat staleness detection. Visual Design (Gemini) â€” thematic handles colors, reactable replaces DT, card_header titles, lazy rendering, SVG, selective plotly, client-side tab switching. Statistical Corrections (DeepSeek) â€” 3 CRITICAL fixes (prediction interval, residual plot x-axis, CI number lines), plus 8 additional corrections (shading, dot plot, mosaic, bin widths, CLT warnings, p-value annotations, stat_function resolution, two-tailed shading). 14 explicit tradeoff decisions documented where reviewers disagreed.

task_summary: CC analyzes dependency graph of its own Codex prompts and identifies parallelization: Phase 1 solo â†’ Phases 2-11 in parallel â†’ Phase 12 last
routing_reason: User asked if phases can run in parallel. CC traced actual file dependencies across all 13 prompts and identified that each creates independent render_unitN.R files. Only conflict point is app.R wiring (placeholder line replacements â€” different lines, trivial merges).

outcome.notes: CC identified the real dependency graph: Phase 0 â†’ Phase 1 (foundation) â†’ Phases 2-11 (parallelizable, each creates own file) â†’ Phase 12 (cutover, needs all done). Offered two grouping strategies: aggressive (all 10 in parallel) and conservative (Batch A: simple charts 2-5, Batch B: distribution curves 6-10, Pipeline 11 anytime). Key insight: each prompt is self-contained with patterns embedded inline, so Codex doesn't need to see prior unit code. Only merge conflict risk: app.R placeholder replacements (trivial) and shared utils.R helpers.

parallelization_model.merge_risk: low â€” app.R has different placeholder lines per phase, trivial to merge
parallelization_model.cc_recommendation: Run Phase 1 solo, then all 2-11 in parallel, merge sequentially

automation_implication: The phase runner design must support both sequential and parallel execution. Git branching strategy needed: each parallel Codex instance works on a separate branch, then merge sequentially into main.

task_summary: Parallel execution hazards observed: app.R last-writer-wins, API signature drift, duplicate Phase 10 output

outcome.notes: Three distinct hazards from running 10 Codex instances on the same branch simultaneously.

last_writer_wins.description: All 10 instances modified app.R to wire their unit. Whichever instance wrote last 'won'. Result: units 1, 6, 7, 8, 9 were wired; units 2, 3, 4, 5 and Pipeline remained as placeholders despite their render files existing.
last_writer_wins.mitigation: CC rewrote app.R post-merge. Future automation should use per-branch execution + sequential merge.

api_signature_drift.description: Units 1-4 used minimal module signature: (id, pane_data). Units 5-9 used full signature: (id, pane_data, heartbeat, has_first_tick, active_tab/is_active). No shared convention emerged because instances couldn't see each other's code.
api_signature_drift.mitigation: CC's app.R rewrite matched each module's actual exported signature. Phase 12 (cutover) or future refactor could normalize.
api_signature_drift.root_cause: Per-phase prompts specified the module API pattern but left room for interpretation. Earlier phases (simpler units) used simpler APIs; later phases (distribution/inference units) needed more context arguments.

duplicate_phase.description: Phase 10 (Unit 9) produced TWO separate outputs from two different Codex instances. Both implemented the same fixes (CI/PI, residual x-axis, t-curve). Likely user error in tab assignment or one instance finished early and was re-fed.
duplicate_phase.mitigation: CC used whichever was last on disk. No harm done since both implemented the same spec.

automation_lessons: Per-branch execution is non-negotiable for parallel Codex â€” all-on-master creates last-writer-wins on shared files
Module API signatures should be locked in the prompt (exact function signature, not just pattern)
Phase assignment must be tracked to prevent duplicate execution
A manifest/lock file could prevent two runners from claiming the same phase

task_summary: Codex Phase 2 instance detected concurrent file modifications and asked for user direction instead of blindly overwriting

outcome.notes: The Phase 2 (Unit 1) Codex instance noticed that app.R already contained Unit 9 wiring (from a faster-finishing Phase 10 instance). Instead of silently overwriting, it paused and asked: 'Do you want me to keep those concurrent Unit 9 changes and only guarantee Unit 1 integration, or reset app.R strictly to Phase 2 scope?' This shows situational awareness â€” Codex detected unexpected state and escalated rather than proceeding blindly.

codex_behavior.awareness_type: noticed another instance's modifications in a shared file

profile_implication: Codex has some ability to detect when its execution context has been altered by concurrent processes. This is a STRENGTH for parallel execution â€” it won't silently destroy another instance's work (at least when changes are obvious).

phase_11_details.recompute_flow: tool/slider change â†’ debounce 300ms â†’ POST overrides â†’ update snapshot â†’ 429 busy handling with 2s retry

task_summary: User identifies the fan-out/fan-in dispatch pattern as isomorphic to grid-bot trading slots and StarCraft Protoss probe harvesting

outcome.notes: User recognized the abstract dispatch pattern connecting three systems: (1) grid-bot-v2 trading slots â€” deploy capital to grid levels, slots fill independently, return profit to pool for rebalancing, (2) StarCraft Protoss probes â€” warp in from nexus, harvest minerals autonomously, return to nexus for resource allocation, (3) Codex parallel instances â€” CC dispatches phase prompts, Codex produces modules independently, CC evaluates and integrates at the hub. The common shape: fan-out to independent workers â†’ each produces autonomously â†’ fan-in to central hub for evaluation and integration. The hub (nexus/CC/pool) never does the harvesting â€” it dispatches and judges.

pattern.name: dispatch-harvest-evaluate
invariants: Hub dispatches but does not produce
Workers produce but do not evaluate
All products return to hub for quality judgment
Hub integrates products into unified state (portfolio / build queue / app.R)

design_implication: This pattern should inform the automation runner design. The runner IS the nexus. It dispatches prompts (warps probes), monitors completion (watches harvest), and triggers CC evaluation (returns to nexus). The pattern is already proven in the user's own trading bot.

task_summary: Railway Docker build fails at step 10/14 â€” setuptools backend import error. CC diagnoses root cause and writes Codex fix prompt.

outcome.notes: First Railway deployment attempt. Docker build progresses through steps 1-9 (base image, apt packages, Python 3.13 from deadsnakes PPA, venv, file copies) but fails at step 10 (pip install). Root cause: pyproject.toml specifies build-backend = 'setuptools.backends._legacy:_Backend' which requires setuptools >=77.0, but requires = ['setuptools>=75.0'] â€” pip installs 75.x which doesn't have that module. CC diagnosed this immediately from the traceback and wrote both a spec (DOCKER_FIX_SPEC.md) and a Codex prompt (specs/codex/docker-fix.md) with 4 targeted fixes.

root_cause.issue: build-backend = 'setuptools.backends._legacy:_Backend' requires setuptools >=77.0 but requires = ['setuptools>=75.0'] only guarantees 75.x
root_cause.secondary: pip 26.0.1 is stricter about build isolation than earlier versions

changes.fix: New file â€” exclude .git, specs, tests
Pin pip range pip>=24,<27 + add setuptools>=68

cc_fix_plan.routing: CC diagnosed â†’ CC wrote spec + Codex prompt â†’ Codex will execute fix

gpt_prediction_vindicated: GPT Deep Research (obs #7) flagged Docker/Railway build risks as a top concern: version mismatches, build-timeout constraints. This is exactly the class of issue GPT predicted.

task_summary: CC debugs Railway deployment over multiple sessions â€” downloads R, Railway CLI, Docker locally. Root cause: Railway dashboard custom start command overriding Dockerfile CMD. Fix: railway.toml startCommand.
routing_reason: Extended debugging session. CC needed to reproduce the environment locally â€” downloaded R, Railway CLI, and Docker to assist. No other LLM involved.
context_given: Railway build logs, Docker config, supervisord config, railway.toml. CC had full file access to grid-bot-v3.

outcome.notes: CC diagnosed a config-precedence issue that was invisible in the codebase. Railway's dashboard-level custom start command (python -m infra.main) silently overrode the Dockerfile CMD, so supervisord and Shiny never started â€” only the Python bot ran as PID 1. Fix: added startCommand to railway.toml, which takes priority over dashboard settings per Railway's config-as-code rules. Dashboard now live at web-production-c44ec.up.railway.app, healthcheck passing, both processes running under supervisord.

cc_capabilities_demonstrated.tool_installation: Downloaded R, Railway CLI, and Docker to Windows to reproduce the deployment environment locally
cc_capabilities_demonstrated.config_precedence_reasoning: Identified that Railway's config hierarchy (railway.toml > dashboard settings > Dockerfile CMD) was the root cause â€” not a code bug
cc_capabilities_demonstrated.persistence: Multiple sessions over several hours to isolate the issue
cc_capabilities_demonstrated.infrastructure_debugging: Diagnosed process supervision, port binding, healthcheck, and container lifecycle issues

gpt_prediction_update: GPT Deep Research (obs #7) predicted Docker/Railway deployment issues â€” partially vindicated. GPT flagged version mismatches and build constraints; actual root cause was config precedence (a different class of deployment issue). GPT's general prediction was correct (deployment WILL be tricky), but the specific failure mode was unpredicted.

deployment_status.supervisord: PID 1, managing python bot + R Shiny
deployment_status.python_bot: running (EOrder:Insufficient funds â€” needs account funding)
deployment_status.shiny_dashboard: running on port 8080, serving HTML framework

task_summary: CC fixes allowReconnect crash â€” Shiny version in Docker doesn't export it. Server function died on SockJS connect, UI rendered but all cards empty. Commit 9685b24.
routing_reason: Continuation of deployment debugging. CC diagnosed from Railway logs.

outcome.notes: shiny::allowReconnect(TRUE) on line 92 crashed the server function immediately after SockJS connection. The Shiny version installed in Docker doesn't export allowReconnect. Symptoms: HTML rendered (UI function fine), SockJS connected, but server function died before any reactive outputs fired â€” all cards empty, no data, no spinner. CC also cleaned up two dead-code artifacts: SockJS monkey-patch (ran before SockJS loaded, was a no-op) and debug trace options. Commit 9685b24, pushed to master.

meta_pattern: Same class as obs #26 (setuptools version) and #27 (config precedence). The recurring theme: something silently overrides or fails due to version/config differences between dev environment and Docker container.

task_summary: CC provides architectural advisory â€” advises against Haskell rewrite, identifies 'silent override' meta-pattern across all deployment bugs, recommends health endpoint as highest-value refactor
routing_reason: User asked strategic architecture questions. CC had full context from debugging sessions.

outcome.notes: Two advisory exchanges. (1) Haskell question: CC correctly identified that all bugs have been infrastructure/config, not logic â€” Haskell's type system wouldn't have caught any of them. Practical concerns: third language, steep curve, slow GHC builds, heavy binaries, poor Kraken library support. Acknowledged where Haskell WOULD help (newtype wrappers for currency safety) but concluded Python+tests achieves same safety with less friction. (2) Refactor question: CC identified the meta-pattern ('something silently overrides something else') across all deployment bugs and recommended a health/status endpoint on the Python bot's internal API (8081) as highest-value refactor. Proposed specific JSON schema. Secondary recommendation: single-process architecture (drop supervisord+Shiny, serve dashboard directly from Python) but only if Shiny keeps causing deployment headaches.

cc_advisory_characteristics.pattern_recognition: Identified meta-pattern across 4+ deployment bugs: silent overrides
cc_advisory_characteristics.strategic_restraint: Advised AGAINST a rewrite (Haskell), recommended additive change (health endpoint)
cc_advisory_characteristics.tradeoff_analysis: Acknowledged Haskell's theoretical advantages but weighed practical costs
cc_advisory_characteristics.incremental_approach: Small additive fix first (health endpoint), bigger change only if pain continues (drop Shiny)
cc_advisory_characteristics.context_leverage: Used accumulated debugging context to inform architectural recommendations

new_cc_role_data: CC as architectural advisor â€” not just implementer/reviewer but strategic counselor. Draws on accumulated project context to give grounded recommendations. Resists scope creep (said no to Haskell).

task_summary: CC creates SPECIALIST_PROMPTS.md with explicit file manifests per web UI LLM â€” Gemini (12 files/~4100 lines), ChatGPT (22 files/~3980 lines), DeepSeek (24 files/~5810 lines), plus 9 shared AP Stats frameworks
routing_reason: CC formalized the file-selection heuristic that was previously implicit. Each specialist gets only domain-relevant files.

outcome.notes: CC documented which files each web UI specialist needs attached. Gemini: Shiny modules, CSS, theme, PaneData types (visual layer). ChatGPT: state machine, pipeline, infra, Dockerfile, configs (systems layer). DeepSeek: all stats modules, all viz/unit builders, analytics (math layer). All three share the 9 AP Stats framework files for educational context. This codifies the routing knowledge that was previously in CC's head during prompt authoring.

routing_pattern_evolution.before: CC selected files ad-hoc per dispatch, based on intuition about each specialist's domain
routing_pattern_evolution.after: Explicit file manifests documented â€” reusable across future review cycles without CC having to re-derive the selection

task_summary: User identifies manual copy-paste as the biggest friction point in the multi-LLM workflow â€” opening every R file individually, pasting into aistudio.google.com, extracting correct prompt sections
routing_reason: User narrating pain points in the human-in-the-loop dispatch process.

outcome.notes: Design input for automation. The manual steps are: (1) open each file CC specifies in the manifest, (2) copy its contents, (3) paste into web UI attachment/text field (aistudio.google.com for Gemini, etc.), (4) find the correct prompt section in SPECIALIST_PROMPTS.md, (5) copy-paste that portion into the web UI text field. This is repeated per specialist, per review cycle.

friction_analysis.bottleneck: human copy-paste between terminal (CC output) and browser (web UI LLMs)
steps_per_specialist: paste into web UI attachment field
paste prompt into web UI text field
friction_analysis.scale: Gemini needs 12 files attached, DeepSeek needs 24 â€” that's 36+ copy-paste operations for one review cycle
friction_analysis.user_proposed_solution: Browser extension (Edge or Chrome) that can receive data from CC/Codex and fill in web UI text fields and attachment methods directly
alternative_approaches: File-based staging: CC writes files to a shared directory, user drags into browser
Clipboard automation: CC copies to clipboard in sequence, user pastes
Browser extension: programmatic fill of web UI fields from CC data
API access: bypass web UI entirely (blocked for Gemini/ChatGPT/Grok â€” no API keys)

automation_priority: HIGH â€” this is the human bottleneck in the dispatch-harvest-evaluate pattern. The hub (CC) can prepare perfectly, but the human relay between CC and web UI specialists is slow and error-prone.

task_summary: Web UI input capabilities documented per LLM â€” Gemini and ChatGPT support file upload, DeepSeek is paste-only, Grok unknown. CC staging strategy must bifurcate.
routing_reason: User confirmed from direct experience which web UIs accept file uploads vs paste-only. Critical for automation design.

outcome.notes: Profiles updated with web_ui_input field. Two staging strategies identified: (1) file-stagers (Gemini, ChatGPT) â€” CC writes files to staging dir, user drags into upload zone, (2) paste-stagers (DeepSeek) â€” CC concatenates all files with delimiters into single clipboard-ready blob. Grok untested.

automation_implication: CC's dispatch preparation must branch on target LLM input type. For file-uploaders: write individual files to a staging directory. For paste-only: concatenate prompt + all file contents into a single text block with clear delimiters (```filename.ext markers). DeepSeek's paste-only + 24-file manifest (~5810 lines) + long_documents:false = potential context limit risk that needs testing.
open_questions: ChatGPT long_documents:false â€” can it handle 22 files uploaded at once?
DeepSeek context limit â€” does ~6000 lines of pasted code fit?
Grok â€” file upload or paste only?
Do any UIs accept .R files specifically, or do they need .txt extension?

task_summary: CORRECTION â€” DeepSeek and Grok both support file uploads. DeepSeek handles uploads better than Gemini. Gemini is choosy about file types.

outcome.notes: Previous obs #32 was wrong about DeepSeek (paste-only) and incomplete about Grok (unknown). All four web UI LLMs support file upload. Gemini is the pickiest about which files it accepts. DeepSeek is surprisingly better at handling uploads than Gemini. Profiles corrected.

automation_implication: Staging strategy simplifies â€” CC can use file-based staging for ALL four web UIs. No need for paste-blob fallback. The only per-LLM variation is Gemini's file-type pickiness (may need .txt extension or specific formats).

task_summary: Third fan-out review cycle complete â€” Gemini (UI/UX), ChatGPT (Systems+Haskell), DeepSeek (Math/AP Stats). CC synthesizes into HARDENING_SPEC.md. Pattern confirmed 3/3.
routing_reason: Same 3-specialist fan-out pattern as cycles 1 and 2. Third confirmation.

outcome.notes: CC dispatched three specialist review prompts (using the documented file manifests from obs #30), collected all three reviews, and synthesized into docs/HARDENING_SPEC.md. This is the third successful execution of the same fan-out/synthesize pattern. New element: ChatGPT was asked to evaluate Haskell integration (Option B: compile-on-save as third process with JSON IPC) â€” extending GPT's systems-architecture role into language evaluation.

key_items: Nav grouping into 5 menus (AP Stats syllabus structure)
Standard interpretation panel (Trading Context / AP Framework / Bot Action)
Accessibility fixes (contrast, mobile tables, subtext over tooltips)
'What If?' sandbox sliders using existing hypothetical recompute
gemini_ui.pattern_note: Gemini continues to propose educational features beyond what was asked (interpretation panels, AP mnemonics)

key_items: Haskell SM as third process (compile-on-save, JSON IPC on localhost:8082)
Railway Volume for persistence (SQLite + Haskell source + compiled binary)
shinymanager authentication (flagged public dashboard as critical risk)
Composite healthcheck, circuit breaker for Kraken, SQLite retention/maintenance
chatgpt_systems.pattern_note: GPT caught a CRITICAL security issue â€” public dashboard can edit code without auth. Same pattern as obs #7 (catches issues others miss).

deepseek_math.focus: Mathematical soundness and AP Stats alignment
key_items: Independence caveat on every inferential unit (the elephant in the room)
Newey-West SEs side-by-side with naive SEs
Runs test alongside chi-square, ACF/PACF diagnostics, improved effective_n
Full AP Stats interpretation templates for all 9 units with trading context
deepseek_math.pattern_note: DeepSeek continues to find the foundational statistical issue â€” independence assumption violation is exactly the kind of thing only a math specialist catches.

synthesis.structure: 4 phases, 20 items, prioritized by risk reduction
synthesis.cc_prioritization: Security (auth) before correctness (stats) before new capability (Haskell) before aesthetics (polish) â€” sensible risk-reduction ordering

pattern_confirmation.new_element: Haskell evaluation routed to ChatGPT (systems architecture includes language/runtime decisions)
pattern_confirmation.routing_pattern_status: CONFIRMED 3/3 â€” the fan-out to 3 specialists + CC synthesis is now a stable, repeatable workflow

task_summary: CC plans second parallel Codex batch â€” 4 agents with zero file overlap, explicit data contracts between agents, 2-batch dependency graph. Lessons from batch 1 visibly applied.
routing_reason: User asked CC to identify parallelizable implementation work and write Codex prompts. Same role as obs #14 (dependency analysis + prompt authoring).

outcome.notes: CC mapped every spec item to its files, identified zero-overlap file ownership boundaries across 4 agents, and designed a 2-batch execution plan. Key evolution from batch 1: (1) explicit file ownership per agent (no shared-file conflicts), (2) locked data contract between Agent A (Python) and Agent B (R Shiny) â€” exact JSON shape specified in both Python and R, (3) batch 2 depends on merge of batch 1 (integration wiring needs all 4 agents' output).

batch_1.file_conflicts: zero â€” ownership boundaries explicitly mapped

agent_A.name: Python Data Pipeline
agent_A.items: stats hardening, interpretation text, independence warnings

agent_B.name: R Shiny UI
agent_B.items: nav grouping, auth, interpretation panels, accessibility

agent_E.name: Integration wiring
agent_E.items: Haskell IPC into slot_engine, proxy endpoints, shinyAce editor

lessons_applied_from_batch_1.file_ownership: obs #18 hazard (last-writer-wins on app.R) â†’ now explicit zero-overlap file boundaries per agent
lessons_applied_from_batch_1.api_contract: obs #18 hazard (API signature drift) â†’ now explicit JSON data contract between Agent A and B with exact field names and types in both Python and R
lessons_applied_from_batch_1.merge_strategy: obs #20 (CC as merge layer) â†’ batch 2 explicitly depends on CC merging batch 1 first

new_pattern: Inter-agent data contract â€” CC defines the exact data shape that one agent produces and another consumes, specified in BOTH languages. This prevents the API drift seen in batch 1 where parallel agents independently interpreted the API pattern.

task_summary: Hardening batch complete â€” all 3 processes running in production (Python bot, R Shiny, Haskell SM). CC fixed Haskell compilation issues live. Two commits pushed.
routing_reason: CC handled Codex output review, Haskell debugging, and deployment verification in a single extended session.

outcome.notes: All 3 processes confirmed running in production: (1) Python bot trading with profit=$1.80 at tick 24, circuit breaker recovering properly, (2) R Shiny dashboard serving on port 8080, (3) Haskell state machine responding on :8082 with /status, /source, /step endpoints. CC fixed two Haskell issues: closeâ†’closeCh name shadowing (b723e27), missing -rtsopts GHC flag for RTS memory caps (40a8029). healthy:false is expected â€” requires compilation/activation cycle. Python bot has fallback SM implementation so Haskell is non-blocking.

cc_haskell_capability.notes: CC debugged Haskell compilation issues directly â€” not just Python/R. This is the first Haskell-specific debugging data point for CC's profile.

deployment_status.remaining: Kraken account needs funding (not a code issue)

task_summary: User identifies context-window management as a workflow friction point â€” must periodically tell CC to generate a continuation prompt before hitting 90% context
routing_reason: User narrating a recurring pain point in the CC workflow.

outcome.notes: CC sessions have a finite context window. When it approaches 90%, the user must manually ask CC to write a CONTINUATION_PROMPT.md that captures current state, then start a fresh session and paste it in. This is a human-initiated context checkpoint. The friction: (1) user must notice context is getting full, (2) user must interrupt their flow to request the prompt, (3) new session loses all accumulated in-context reasoning â€” only what was explicitly serialized survives. This is the second major workflow friction identified (after copy-paste relay in obs #31).

manual_steps: user asks CC to write continuation prompt
CC serializes current state to CONTINUATION_PROMPT.md
new session rebuilds context from the prompt
friction_analysis.information_loss: accumulated reasoning, nuance, and in-context state that wasn't explicitly captured
friction_analysis.frequency: every extended session â€” the hardening implementation likely required multiple context resets
potential_automation: CC auto-detects 80% context and proactively writes checkpoint
Structured state files that CC reads on startup (not just a prose prompt)
Agent repo itself could serve as the persistent memory layer between sessions

connection_to_agent_repo: The Agent repo's CONTINUATION_PROMPT.md is literally this pattern â€” a serialized context checkpoint. The profiles and observations are a more structured version of the same idea. The Agent repo IS the persistent memory that survives context resets.

task_summary: Phase 4 context persistence layer added to Agent repo with structured state files and startup reconstruction flow.
routing_reason: Direct response to workflow friction in obs #37: context continuity depended on manual continuation prompts only.

outcome.notes: Added `state/session.json` for active project/task/checkpoint timestamp, `state/project-grid-bot-v3.json` for commits/deployment/open issues, and matching schemas. Updated `CLAUDE.md` startup instructions so CC reconstructs context from structured state first, then `CONTINUATION_PROMPT.md`. Added explicit auto-checkpoint policy at 80% context to refresh both structured and prose layers before 90%.

task_summary: Phase 7 defined as long-term browser extension effort: Edge/Chrome extension with native host, staging watcher, and tiered automation rollout.
routing_reason: User proposed the next major dispatch automation phase to remove browser copy-paste friction after file staging.

outcome.notes: Scope captured as a design target only. Core requirements: (1) browser extension + native messaging host, (2) watch staging directory for new bundles, (3) auto-fill text field and attachment zone, (4) three automation tiers from manual trigger to watch mode to full auto.

evidence.obs_31: Copy-paste and file relay are the primary human bottleneck.
evidence.obs_33: All target web UIs support file uploads.

automation_priority: LONG-TERM-HIGH - largest engineering effort after foundational staging workflow.

task_summary: MILESTONE: All 7 automation phases implemented by Codex — file staging, inbound harvest, sequential runner, context persistence, parallel runner, routing intelligence, browser extension design. Dispatched in parallel while Agent-repo CC was still planning.
routing_reason: User dispatched all 7 phases to Codex for implementation while Agent-repo CC session was still in plan mode. Phases 1, 3, 4 ran in parallel (no dependencies); phases 2, 5, 6 followed; phase 7 was design-only.

outcome.notes: All 7 phases implemented and validated. Phase 1: stage-dispatch.ps1 with per-specialist staging dirs, manifest-driven file copies, Gemini .R.txt mirrors. Phase 2: harvest-responses.ps1 with cycle archiving, diffs between cycles, template enforcement. Phase 3: codex-runner.sh with sequential execution, verification, progress tracking, resume on failure, optional CC gate. Phase 4: session.json + project-grid-bot-v3.json + schemas + CLAUDE.md startup instructions. Phase 5: parallel-codex-runner.py with branch-per-agent, file ownership enforcement, contract validation, dependency batching, CC merge pass. Phase 6: route-task.ps1 with confidence-weighted routing, auto-generated file manifests, prompt templates. Phase 7: design doc only (browser extension roadmap).

meta_observation: The Agent-repo CC session was still in plan mode when the user dispatched all 7 implementation prompts to Codex in the grid-bot CC session. This is the dispatch-harvest-evaluate pattern applied recursively — the user used one CC session (grid-bot) to generate prompts while the other CC session (Agent) was doing strategic planning. The user IS the hub, orchestrating two CC sessions + Codex simultaneously.

task_summary: CC designs hooks-integration-spec and 3 parallel Codex prompts to wire routing intelligence natively into CC hooks and Codex config — replacing static scripts with dynamic, event-driven automation
routing_reason: User identified that static PowerShell scripts aren't real automation. CC researched CC hooks (17 events) and Codex config (notify, AGENTS.md, skills), designed native integration.

outcome.notes: Spec at design/hooks-integration-spec.md. Three Codex prompts created: (A) CC hook scripts — SessionStart context injection, Stop auto-staging, PreCompact auto-checkpoint. (B) Codex notify script — updates progress tracking on agent-turn-complete. (C) Grid-bot AGENTS.md + skills — routing-aware project instructions for Codex. All 3 parallel with zero file overlap. CC wires hooks into settings.json after review (Agent D).

hooks_designed.cc_session_start: Injects routing profiles + project state into CC context on startup
hooks_designed.cc_auto_stage: Auto-runs stage-dispatch.ps1 when CC writes specialist prompts (async)
hooks_designed.cc_auto_checkpoint: Auto-saves state on PreCompact (before context compaction)

batch_2: Agent D (CC wires into settings.json + config.toml — done by CC, not Codex)

task_summary: MILESTONE: Hooks integration complete — CC dispatched 3 parallel Codex agents via `codex exec`, all succeeded, CC wired hooks into settings.json and config.toml. First live CC→Codex→CC round-trip.
routing_reason: CC designed the spec, wrote the prompts, launched 3 Codex agents in parallel via `codex exec --full-auto`, reviewed all output, then wired the hooks itself (Agent D). Full dispatch-harvest-evaluate cycle executed from within CC.

outcome.notes: All 4 agents completed successfully. Agent A: 3 bash hook scripts (cc-session-start.sh 225 lines, cc-auto-stage.sh 76 lines, cc-auto-checkpoint.sh 53 lines). Agent B: codex-notify.py 356 lines with atomic writes, phase detection, edge case handling. Agent C: AGENTS.md + 2 skills in grid-bot-v3. Agent D (CC): wired routing hooks alongside existing earcon hooks in settings.json, added notify to config.toml. All hooks validated — SessionStart produces concise routing summary, auto-stage exits silently on non-dispatch, auto-checkpoint updates state, codex-notify tracks phase completions.

execution_details.sequential_agent: Agent D (CC wires settings — done by CC itself, not Codex)
execution_details.dispatch_method: codex exec --full-auto with prompt file piped via stdin

wiring_summary.coexistence: All routing hooks added alongside existing earcon hooks — zero conflicts

session_start_output: Verified: 12-line routing summary injected on startup — roster with roles/confidence, project status, active task, last 3 observations, tool inventory
significance: First time CC directly dispatched Codex agents and integrated the results — closing the dispatch-harvest-evaluate loop entirely within CC. No human relay needed for CC↔Codex work.
